medications.indication_rationale: IVIG is the definitive treatment for Kawasaki disease and must be given promptly (urgently once cardiac involvement is suspected) to reduce coronary aneurysm risk; it is an inpatient therapy and cannot be given or dosed over telehealth.
contraindications_in_this_case: Inpatient administration only - do not dose remotely; no paediatric dose generated in this encounter

medications.indication_rationale: Continuing to alternate paracetamol/ibuprofen at home has not controlled the fever and would delay definitive treatment; parents should stop giving further OTC medicines and get the child to hospital.
contraindications_in_this_case: Do not continue home antipyretics as a management strategy - immediate transfer is required

integrative_alternative_therapies.therapy_name: Managing the fever at home overnight with fluids and antipyretics
integrative_alternative_therapies.rationale: Delays time-critical IVIG; untreated Kawasaki risks coronary aneurysms and myocardial infarction.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Stop giving further over-the-counter medicines and prepare for immediate hospital transfer.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Home antipyretics are ineffective here and delay definitive care.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Hold and comfort the child in a calm, cool environment; do not force fluids or food if he resists (aspiration risk).
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Keeping the child calm reduces heart rate and metabolic demand and makes transfer safer.

patient_education_points.point: A fever lasting more than five days with red eyes, cracked lips, a rash and swollen hands and feet is a serious condition called Kawasaki disease, which can inflame the blood vessels around the heart. With the new lethargy and chest clutching, this is an emergency.
Please call 000 for an ambulance now to take him to a children's emergency department for an urgent heart scan and a special intravenous medicine (IVIG) to protect his heart.

Stop giving any more over-the-counter fever medicines, keep him calm and cool, and don't force food or drink if he resists. If he becomes unresponsive, turns blue, or stops breathing, start CPR and keep 000 on the line.
Getting the intravenous treatment promptly greatly reduces the chance of permanent heart damage - that's why he needs to go now, not tomorrow.

follow_up_plan.interval: Immediate hospital admission; long-term paediatric cardiology follow-up
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Echocardiographic coronary artery status and cardiac function, response to IVIG (and any second-line therapy), inflammatory markers, and long-term coronary surveillance and antithrombotic management
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Unresponsiveness, colour change, or cessation of breathing while waiting - start CPR and keep 000 on the line.

must_include_items: Recognition of possible/complete Kawasaki disease (prolonged fever + mucocutaneous/extremity signs)
Recognition of cardiac red flags (lethargy, chest clutching, disproportionate tachycardia/new murmur)
Immediate ambulance (000) transfer to a paediatric emergency department for echocardiography and IVIG
Instruction to stop home antipyretics and keep the child calm/cool without forcing fluids
acceptable_alternatives: 'Kawasaki disease', 'serious inflammation of the blood vessels affecting the heart' are acceptable framings
Immediate ambulance or, if unavailable, urgent parent-driven transfer to a paediatric ED - but ambulance is preferred given possible cardiac involvement
errors_of_omission: Kawasaki disease not recognised / dismissed as a virus
No advice to stop home antipyretics / no CPR safety-netting
errors_of_commission: Advising continued home antipyretics and overnight observation
Reassuring the parents it is a simple viral illness
Forcing fluids/food in a distressed child (aspiration risk)
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: CRITICAL PAEDIATRIC EMERGENCY: A pass requires (1) recognising possible Kawasaki disease with cardiac involvement and (2) arranging immediate ambulance transfer for IVIG/echocardiography. Advising home antipyretics/observation or a routine review is a dangerous, automatic-fail under-triage risking coronary aneurysms and death.
